questionText: A curve has parametric equations $x = 2t + 1$ and $y = t^{2} - t - 6$. Find the coordinates of the points where the curve crosses the $x$-axis.

steps.description: State the condition for the x-axis
steps.explanation: Every point on the $x$-axis has zero height, so the curve meets it exactly where the $y$-coordinate is zero.

steps.description: Set the y equation to zero
steps.explanation: Only the $y$-coordinate must vanish, so we work with the $y$ equation. This gives a quadratic in $t$.
Numbers multiplying to $-6$ and adding to $-1$ are $-3$ and $+2$.
These are the parameter values at which the curve reaches the axis. Both are real and unrestricted, so both are kept.
With $t$ known, the matching $x$-coordinate comes straight from the $x$ equation.
Pairing this $x$ with $y = 0$ gives the first crossing point.
Repeating the substitution for the second parameter value.
This is the other point where the curve cuts the $x$-axis.
The curve crosses the $x$-axis at these two points.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: The curve crosses the $x$-axis at $(7,\,0)$ (when $t = 3$) and $(-3,\,0)$ (when $t = -2$).
